For whole cakes half should me content. 2. Kar de, sās, chūlhṛā niyārā, ab toh saṅg na hoë gujārā . Rus. song. Another kitchen, mother, give! longer with you I cannot live. 3. Ke khāgī tauṅh niyārī ho-kar, kimaiṅ kār tane āve nā? Bhāj aṛe se pīhar-roī! man ne muṅh dikhlāve nā! Rus. song. What will you have to eat from me apart? No work you 're fit to do, in nothing smart, Begone, house-mourner, take yourself away! Dont let me see your face again, I pray! niyārā rahnā , v. n. To live apart. niyārā k. , v. a. See alag k . 1, 2, 5, 7, 8, 12—14. niyārā h. , v. n. See alag h . niyāre niyāre, niyārā niyārā , adv. Separately; item by item.
n. m. The scoria left after refining gold, silver, or other metals, from which a minute portion of those metals is obtainable.

ety: the twenty-ninth of the Persian, and twenty-fifth of the Arabic
def: s.m. The letter, or the sound, na.
more: The 25th letter of the Arabic and 29th of the Persian alphabet, corresponds to न in Sanskrit. It is one of the dental letters, and has the sound of the English n , with the following exceptions. 1. When it is quiescent in the middle or end of a word, and follows a madda or simple long vowel it has a very slight nasal sound; as, in zubūṅ bad, zamāṅ time, zamiṅ earth, chāṅd moon. This is called nūn-i-gunnā . If however, the word at the end of which it occurs be so placed in composition that the nūn becomes movable by izāfat it ceases to be nasal and resumes its original sound as a dental letter. 2. When it precedes ب or پ it has the sound of m as in mimbar a pulpit, sampat riches. 3. Before ک or گ it assumes a nasal sound similar to that which the English n has before the corresponding letters in the words 'song, sunk,' as in aṅgāshtan to suppose, Laṅkā, aṅgārā . According to abjad , q. v.…

The twenty-fifth letter of the Arabic and twenty-ninth of the Persian alphabet, corresponds to न in Sanskrit. It is one of the dental letters, and has the sound of the English n, with the following exceptions. 1. When it is quiescent in the middle or end of a word, and follows a madda or simple long vowel, it has a very slight nasal sound; as, in زبون Bad, زمان Time, زمين The earth, ماندن To remain: this is called نون غنب nūn-i-g̠ẖunna. If, however, the word at the end of which it occurs be so placed in composition that the ن become moveable by iẓāfat, it ceases to be nasal and resumes its original sound as a dental letter. 2. When it precedes ب or پ it has the sound of m; as, in منبر mimbar, A pulpit; سنپت sampat, Riches. 3. Before ک or گ it assumes a nasal sound similar to that which the English n has before the corresponding letters in the words song, sunk, as, in انگاشتن angāshtan, T…
